sources.notes: Statement and constraints checked: contiguous subarrays, n<k<2n, values in [1,n], and total n at most 2e5.
Official editorial and embedded code checked for uniqueness of the extremum pair, the value sweep, activated-position segment trees, endpoint capacities, and binary search.
The CF2064F section identifies the monotonic-stack boundary reduction, complementary min/max values, interval overlap test, product contribution, and Fenwick aggregation.
Used for the four nearest-extremum boundaries and the canonical first-prefix-minimum and last-suffix-maximum witness formulation.
Cross-checked the unique x and k-x classification and the official-style grouped-position contribution calculation.
Re-derived the weighted witness-pair formula and compared it against direct enumeration of every subarray and cut on randomized instances up to n=9.

reference_candidates.notes: The solution index did not expose readable bodies; the readable Luogu column above was used instead.
Accepted implementation linked by the monotonic-stack blog; direct source access was bot-protected, so the article, official implementation, and independent derivation were used.

annotation.summary: Count contiguous subarrays that admit a cut where the minimum on the non-empty left part plus the maximum on the non-empty right part equals k.
annotation.constraints: Across test cases, sum(n) is at most 2e5; 2<=n<=2e5, n<k<2n, and 1<=a_i<=n. The answer fits in 64-bit signed arithmetic.
annotation.core_idea: As a cut moves right, both its prefix minimum and suffix maximum are non-increasing. Therefore, if a subarray has one or more valid cuts, every valid cut has the same unique pair (x,y) with x+y=k. Canonicalize an x-epic subarray by i, the first occurrence witnessing prefix minimum x, and j, the last occurrence witnessing suffix maximum y. Let L_le(i) be the nearest index left of i with value <=x, R_lt(i) the nearest index right of i with value <x, L_gt(j) the nearest index left of j with value >y, and R_ge(j) the nearest index right of j with value >=y. For i<j and a_i+a_j=k, a valid cut exists exactly when L_gt(j)<R_lt(i). This witness pair contributes (i-L_le(i))*(R_ge(j)-j) choices of subarray endpoints. Monotonic stacks compute all four boundaries, and complementary-value witness pairs can be aggregated in O(n log n).
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory per test case.
tricks: prove that every epic subarray has a unique prefix-minimum and suffix-maximum value pair
choose the first x witness and last y witness to avoid double counting
reduce cut existence to overlap of two extremum-validity intervals
factor each witness pair into independent left-endpoint and right-endpoint counts
aggregate complementary-value pairs with range-query data structures

solution_variants.name: official-value-sweep-segment-tree
solution_variants.summary: Group positions by value and sweep x upward with y=k-x. Segment trees over activated values locate the first value below x after each x occurrence and the last value above y before each y occurrence. Precompute each y occurrence's valid right-endpoint capacity, binary-search the valid prefix of y occurrences for every x witness, and multiply by its valid left-endpoint count.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory.

solution_variants.name: four-boundary-fenwick-pairing
solution_variants.summary: Use monotonic stacks to compute L_le, R_lt, L_gt, and R_ge. For every complementary value pair x,y, merge-scan their occurrence lists by position. Insert an earlier x witness i into a Fenwick tree at key R_lt(i) with weight i-L_le(i); for each y witness j, query keys greater than L_gt(j) and multiply by R_ge(j)-j. Query before insertion when x=y, and roll back inserted weights after the pair.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory.

tags.evidence: The problem is reduced to the monotone prefix minimum and suffix maximum associated with every possible cut.
Assigning each subarray its unique value pair, first x witness, and last y witness prevents multiple valid cuts or repeated extrema from being counted more than once.
The official solution dynamically locates the first smaller-than-x position and last greater-than-y position among values activated by the value sweep.
The alternative solution requires tie-correct nearest <=, <, >, and >= boundaries to delimit every witness's valid endpoint and cut ranges.
Weighted earlier x witnesses are indexed by R_lt(i), and suffix queries count exactly those satisfying L_gt(j)<R_lt(i).
For each x witness, the official solution binary-searches the last y occurrence whose last-greater boundary remains before the first-smaller boundary.
The Fenwick variant scans occurrence positions in order while maintaining exactly the earlier x witnesses eligible to pair with the current y witness.
Each canonical witness pair contributes a product of independent left and right endpoint counts, which is accumulated through weighted range queries.
Although present among the official tags, neither reviewed solution needs a dynamic-programming state; both use canonical witness counting.
Occurrence lists may be merge-scanned with two cursors, but this is bookkeeping around the boundary and Fenwick argument rather than the central technique.
